Region‑adaptable retrieval of coastal BGC parameters

Physics‑aware meta‑learning from near‑surface hyperspectral remote sensing reflectance

Yiqing Guo et al. • CSIRO AquaWatch
Motivation

Coastal water quality needs continuous, transferable monitoring.

Hyperspectral in situ sensing can retrieve key biogeochemical parameters, but retrieval models often fail when moved across regions.

  • Different catchments create different optical signatures.
  • Local calibration samples are costly and sparse.
  • Purely empirical models do not encode water‑optics physics.
InputsRrs spectra, 400–700 nm
TargetsTSS, DOC, TChl‑a
GoalGeneralise across coastal water bodies
Main contribution

Combine physical realism with regional adaptability.

1

Bio‑optical forward model

Generate synthetic, physically plausible Rrs–BGC pairs.

2

Meta‑learn base model

Pretrain a region‑agnostic model to learn broad physical relationships.

3

Fine‑tune by region

Adapt to local bio‑optical characteristics with limited in situ samples.

Takeaway: physics provides a robust starting point; local data provides site specificity.

Study design

Five Australian coastal sites across tropical, subtropical, and temperate waters.

SiteSamplesInfluence
Fitzroy Estuary296High river
Keppel Bay377Moderate river
Boston Bay774None
Cockburn Sound172Low river
Lucinda Jetty104Moderate river
Measurements

Autonomous sensors link optical spectra to water quality.

  • HydraSpectra recorded water‑leaving Rrs at 15‑minute intervals.
  • YSI EXO2 and OPUS sensors measured BGC variables at 10‑minute intervals.
  • Quality control removed glint, bad weather, wildlife, and low‑signal spectra.
  • Lucinda Jetty used HyperOCR and grab‑sample records from IMOS/AODN.
Method

Two‑stage physics‑aware meta‑learning pipeline

Synthetic data creation → base model pretraining → region‑specific adaptation → BGC retrieval.

Physical plausibility

Synthetic samples are shaped by measured bio‑optical ranges.

  • The bio‑optical spectral library supplies realistic BGC, IOP, SIOP, temperature, and salinity ranges.
  • The forward model converts constituent properties into Rrs spectra.
  • Distribution and correlation checks show alignment with library data.
What controls Rrs?

Sensitivity varies by parameter and wavelength.

  • TSS mainly acts through particulate backscattering.
  • DOC is linked to CDOM absorption.
  • TChl‑a is tied to phytoplankton absorption features.

This justifies using full hyperspectral structure rather than a few empirical bands.

Observed variability

Sites differ in BGC ranges and optical water types.

  • River‑influenced sites show more variable sediment and DOC dynamics.
  • Temperate, lower‑river sites show narrower TSS ranges.
  • Distinct chromaticity and clustering patterns indicate region‑specific Rrs–BGC relationships.
Overall results

Good agreement between predicted and measured BGC.

TSSr² 0.916RMSE 1.67 • MAE 1.45
DOCr² 0.539RMSE 1.52 • MAE 1.36
TChl‑ar² 0.683RMSE 1.60 • MAE 1.41

Axes in the paper are shown on log10 scales.

Regional evaluation

Errors vary by site, reflecting different bio‑optical regimes.

Lower TSS errors: Boston Bay and Cockburn Sound.

Higher variability: river‑influenced Fitzroy, Keppel, and Lucinda.

TChl‑a hardest at Lucinda Jetty.

Comparison with five benchmarks

The proposed model delivers the best overall retrieval accuracy.

VariableBest benchmark r²Proposed r²Proposed RMSEMain gain
TSS0.910.921.67Best error metrics
DOC0.170.541.52Large r² improvement
TChl‑a0.590.681.60Lower bias/RMSE/MAE

Interpretation: physics‑aware pretraining improves robustness; regional fine‑tuning improves local fit.

Operational potential

Predictions track temporal dynamics from continuous Rrs.

Predicted BGC time series align with measured changes in magnitude and timing across sites, with gaps mainly from sensor malfunction or QC removal.

Conclusion

A scalable route to coastal water‑quality intelligence.

  • Retrieves TSS, DOC, and TChl‑a from near‑surface hyperspectral Rrs.
  • Works across five bio‑optically distinct Australian coastal sites.
  • Outperforms empirical and deep learning benchmarks.
  • Supports continuous time‑series monitoring from autonomous sensing.

Next steps

Validate across more optical water types, extend to satellite hyperspectral imagery, and explore optically inactive nutrients with ancillary predictors.
